nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; most commonly found in areas with rock or scattered coral, at depths of 0–40 m. Non-migratory.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on crustaceans and small fish in the wild. In aquariums, generally accepts a variety of meaty foods including frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped seafood, and small live prey.

avoidWith: small fish that can be swallowed

summary: The Harlequin Bass (Serranus tigrinus) is a boldly patterned carnivore from the Caribbean and Western Atlantic, generally considered one of the hardier marine fish in the trade. It tends toward semi-aggressive behavior and will prey on small fish and crustaceans, so tankmate selection requires care.
careNotes:
The Harlequin Bass is generally considered one of the hardier marine fish available in the hobby, making it relatively accessible even for less experienced saltwater keepers. It tends to do best in aquariums furnished with plenty of live rock, caves, and rocky overhangs that provide shelter and territorial anchor points. As it grows, it has been known to prey on small fish and crustaceans small enough to swallow, so tank companions should be chosen accordingly — smaller ornamental shrimp and diminutive fish are at risk.

Most sources advise keeping only one Harlequin Bass per aquarium unless a bonded pair can be obtained, as conspecific aggression tends to be high and individuals have been reported to fight vigorously with members of their own species.

This species is a simultaneous hermaphrodite — each individual possesses both functional male and female reproductive organs at the same time. In the wild, Harlequin Bass have been documented forming monogamous home-ranging pairs, though they do not appear to defend exclusive territories in the way many reef fish do.
breedingNotes: Very little has been reported on captive breeding of the Harlequin Bass. In the wild, the species forms monogamous pairs and spawns pelagically — releasing eggs and sperm into the open water column. No reliable accounts of captive spawning have appeared in the hobbyist literature.

fish.breedingNotes: Very little has been reported on captive breeding. The species forms monogamous pairs in the wild and spawns pelagically, releasing eggs and sperm into open water. No captive-breeding success has been documented in the hobbyist literature.
